title: Course Orientation

learningObjectives.text: Explain how to use the pathway using a new example and justify the result.
Explain expectations and study approach using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: This orientation establishes how to use the JavaScript pathway deliberately. It explains the order of study, retrieval practice, and how mistakes become evidence for review rather than failure.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **How to use the pathway** → **Expectations and study approach**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: How to use the pathway
sections.body: Follow the pathway in order unless a diagnostic or prior experience gives a clear reason to skip. Read the objective before the lesson, answer checks without looking back, and use explanations to repair the mental model. Opening a page is not completion; the learner should be able to retrieve and apply the critical ideas.

sections.title: Expectations and study approach
sections.body: Programming is cumulative. Short, frequent sessions with active recall are usually more effective than passive marathons. Type and modify examples, predict outcomes before revealing them, and record recurring misconceptions.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: Course Orientation connects How to use the pathway, Expectations and study approach.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: A team is making a decision involving Course Orientation. Which approach shows the strongest understanding of **How to use the pathway**?

choices.label: State the requirement and assumptions, apply the How to use the pathway model, and verify the observable consequence before adding complexity.
choices.feedback: Transferable understanding of How to use the pathway means using the model to make and verify a decision in a new context.

choices.label: Use the term How to use the pathway in documentation without changing any decision or behavior.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Choose the option with the most tools even when the requirement is smaller.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Ignore prerequisites and rely on a memorized sentence from the lesson.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: Transferable understanding of How to use the pathway means using the model to make and verify a decision in a new context.
questions.hint: Select the option that connects a mental model to evidence rather than vocabulary alone.

questions.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes **How to use the pathway** from **Expectations and study approach** in this topic?

choices.label: They address different parts of the topic's model; a correct decision identifies which one explains the current requirement and how the two interact.
choices.feedback: The concepts are related but should be applied according to the requirement and the role each plays in Course Orientation.

choices.label: They are interchangeable labels, so choosing between them has no effect on reasoning.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

choices.label: How to use the pathway is always correct and Expectations and study approach is always an error.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

choices.label: The distinction matters only for the exact example used in the lesson.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: The concepts are related but should be applied according to the requirement and the role each plays in Course Orientation.
questions.hint: Look for a distinction based on responsibilities or consequences, not an absolute rule.

questions.prompt: A developer notices this recurring mistake: “Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.” What is the best response?

choices.label: Return to the requirement, use the Course Orientation model to identify the failed assumption, and verify the corrected behavior with a representative case.
choices.feedback: The useful response corrects both the misconception and the next development action for Course Orientation.

choices.label: Hide the symptom and continue so the schedule is not affected.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Add unrelated abstractions before reproducing the problem.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Treat the mistake as unavoidable and remove the relevant check.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: The useful response corrects both the misconception and the next development action for Course Orientation.
questions.hint: Choose the response that makes the failure reproducible and the correction verifiable.

questions.prompt: Which day-to-day workflow is most reliable when a task depends on Course Orientation?

choices.label: Clarify the requirement, identify the relevant model or boundary, test the smallest representative case, then refactor after the behavior is understood.
choices.feedback: A reliable workflow makes assumptions and failures visible early, then improves the implementation after correctness is established.

choices.label: Start with the largest available abstraction and postpone defining expected behavior.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Copy a solution from another project without checking whether its assumptions match.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Optimize for line count first and inspect correctness only after release.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: A reliable workflow makes assumptions and failures visible early, then improves the implementation after correctness is established.
questions.hint: Prefer the sequence that produces evidence early and keeps decisions reversible.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best engineering judgment about Course Orientation?

choices.label: Use Course Orientation when its model clarifies the requirement or trade-off; do not force it into a problem where a simpler explanation is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Use Course Orientation when its model clarifies the requirement or trade-off; do not force it into a problem where a simpler explanation is sufficient.

choices.label: Use Course Orientation in every file because more abstraction always improves software.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Avoid Course Orientation completely because every additional concept reduces maintainability.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Choose only by the number of lines and ignore correctness, ownership, and future change.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Course Orientation rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: Use Course Orientation when its model clarifies the requirement or trade-off; do not force it into a problem where a simpler explanation is sufficient.
questions.hint: Choose the option that balances correctness, clarity, cost, and the actual requirement.

researchSources.title: Watch Before You Start!
researchSources.usage: Coverage research only; no transcript wording is canonical.

contentAuthority.canonical: New JS2Next Release 1 curriculum and independently authored content.
contentAuthority.legacyProjectContent: Supplementary internal reference only; excluded from runtime unless explicitly re-authored and approved.
contentAuthority.courseTranscripts: Coverage and sequencing research only; never learner-facing authority.

assessmentProfile.label: Conceptual and mental-model review
assessmentProfile.rationale: The topic is primarily definitions, mental models, comparisons, or framework purpose. Scenario MCQs are more appropriate than forced code trivia.
assessmentProfile.inLessonPolicy: Use concise concept checks; coding and hybrid topics include at least one code-reading check.
assessmentProfile.topicQuizPolicy: Questions Q04–Q08 form the topic quiz. The presentation mix follows this profile.
